source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Late blight of tomato and potato is caused by Phytophthora infestans, an oomycete (a water mould rather than a true fungus). Symptoms begin as water-soaked grey-green lesions that rapidly enlarge into brown necrotic patches, often with white sporulation on the underside of infected leaves during humid weather. Fruit and tubers may also become infected, leading to rapid crop losses. The disease develops under cool, wet conditions, typically between 15 and 21 degrees Celsius, and spreads through windborne sporangia and infected plant material. Management includes regular crop scouting, prompt removal of infected plants, good field ventilation, avoiding overhead irrigation, and planting resistant cultivars where available. Because the pathogen is an oomycete, effective control requires fungicides specifically active against water moulds, applied preventively before infection periods and rotated according to their mode of action to reduce resistance development. In Germany, only plant-protection products currently authorised by the Federal Office of Consumer Protection and Food Safety (BVL) may legally be used.

source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Early blight of tomato and potato is caused mainly by Alternaria solani, a fungal pathogen that commonly infects older leaves first. Typical symptoms include dark brown circular lesions with distinctive concentric rings, giving a target-like appearance, followed by yellowing and premature leaf drop. Stems and fruits may also develop dark sunken lesions. The disease is favoured by warm temperatures, frequent rainfall or irrigation, prolonged leaf wetness, and plant stress. The fungus survives in infected crop debris and spreads through wind, rain splash, and contaminated equipment. Management includes crop rotation, removal of infected residues, maintaining adequate plant nutrition, reducing leaf wetness through good irrigation practices, and using resistant varieties where available. Preventive fungicide applications are most effective when disease-favourable conditions occur, and fungicide groups should be rotated to minimise resistance. In Germany, only BVL-authorised fungicides may legally be applied.

source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Apple scab is caused by Venturia inaequalis, one of the most economically important fungal diseases of apple orchards in Germany. Symptoms include olive-green to dark brown velvety lesions on leaves and fruits, eventually leading to cracked, deformed, and unmarketable apples. The pathogen overwinters in fallen leaves and releases airborne spores during spring under cool, wet weather conditions. Infection is favoured by extended periods of leaf wetness and moderate temperatures. Management includes removing infected leaf litter, improving canopy airflow through pruning, planting resistant cultivars where suitable, and monitoring infection periods using disease forecasting systems. Preventive fungicide programmes are commonly used during susceptible growth stages, with rotation between fungicide groups to reduce resistance risk. Only plant-protection products authorised by the BVL may be used in Germany.

source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Septoria leaf spot of tomato is caused by Septoria lycopersici, a fungal pathogen that primarily affects leaves while rarely infecting fruits. Symptoms appear as numerous small circular grey or tan lesions with dark margins and tiny black fruiting bodies in the centre. Severe infections cause extensive leaf yellowing, premature defoliation, and reduced fruit yield and quality. The disease develops under warm, humid conditions with prolonged leaf wetness and spreads through rain splash, irrigation water, and contaminated tools. Management includes removing infected foliage, rotating crops, avoiding overhead irrigation, increasing air circulation, and using disease-free planting material. Preventive fungicide applications may be required during periods favourable for infection, following resistance-management recommendations. In Germany, growers must use only fungicides currently authorised by the BVL.

pathogen: Passalora fulva (syn. Fulvia fulva, Cladosporium fulvum)
source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Leaf mould of tomato is caused by Passalora fulva, formerly known as Fulvia fulva (and earlier Cladosporium fulvum), a fungal pathogen that is particularly important in greenhouse tomato production. Initial symptoms include pale yellow spots on the upper surface of leaves, while olive-green to brown velvety fungal growth develops on the underside. Severe infections result in leaf curling, yellowing, and premature defoliation, reducing fruit production. The disease thrives under high humidity, poor ventilation, and moderate temperatures, especially in protected cultivation. Spores spread through air movement, contaminated clothing, equipment, and plant debris. Management focuses on reducing greenhouse humidity, improving ventilation, removing infected leaves, maintaining good sanitation, and planting resistant tomato varieties where available. Fungicides may be applied preventively when environmental conditions favour disease development, following resistance-management guidelines. In Germany, only BVL-authorised plant-protection products may legally be used.

crops: Squash, Cucumber, Pumpkin, Melon and other cucurbits
source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Powdery mildew of cucurbits is primarily caused by Podosphaera xanthii, a fungal pathogen that infects squash, cucumber, pumpkin, melon and other cucurbit crops. The disease is recognised by white, powdery fungal growth on the upper surfaces of leaves, stems and occasionally fruits. As infection progresses, leaves turn yellow, dry out and die prematurely, reducing photosynthesis, fruit quality and yield. Unlike many fungal diseases, powdery mildew can develop under relatively dry conditions when humidity is moderate to high, making it common in both open fields and greenhouses. The fungus spreads by airborne spores carried over long distances by wind. Management includes planting resistant cultivars where available, ensuring good air circulation, avoiding excessive nitrogen fertilisation, removing infected plant material and monitoring crops regularly. Preventive fungicide applications are most effective before severe infection develops, with rotation between fungicide groups to minimise resistance. In Germany, only plant-protection products currently authorised by the Federal Office of Consumer Protection and Food Safety (BVL) may legally be used.

source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Black rot of grape is caused by Guignardia bidwellii, a fungal pathogen that affects leaves, shoots, tendrils and grape berries. Early symptoms appear as small tan to brown leaf spots with dark margins containing tiny black fruiting bodies. On fruit, infections develop into brown lesions that eventually cause berries to shrivel into hard black mummies. The disease is favoured by warm temperatures, rainfall and prolonged periods of leaf wetness during the growing season. The fungus survives in infected fruit and plant debris and spreads through spores released during wet weather. Management includes removing mummified berries and infected debris, pruning to improve canopy ventilation, monitoring weather conditions and applying preventive fungicides during susceptible growth stages. Resistant grape cultivars may reduce disease severity where available. In Germany, where viticulture is economically important, only BVL-authorised fungicides may legally be applied.

source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Northern corn leaf blight is caused by Exserohilum turcicum, a fungal pathogen that is one of the major foliar diseases of maize. Symptoms consist of long, elliptical grey-green lesions that enlarge into tan cigar-shaped spots on leaves, reducing photosynthetic capacity and grain yield. Severe infections may lead to premature leaf death, particularly when disease develops before grain filling. The pathogen is favoured by moderate temperatures, frequent rainfall, heavy dew and prolonged leaf wetness. It survives on infected maize residues and spreads through windborne spores. Management includes crop rotation, residue management, planting resistant maize hybrids, monitoring disease development and applying fungicides when economically justified. Fungicide groups should be rotated to delay resistance development. In Germany, where maize is widely cultivated, only BVL-authorised fungicides may legally be used.

source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Common rust of maize is caused by Puccinia sorghi, a fungal pathogen that produces numerous small cinnamon-brown pustules on both surfaces of maize leaves. Heavy infections reduce photosynthesis, weaken plant growth and may decrease grain yield, particularly when disease develops early in the season. The disease is favoured by cool to moderate temperatures, high humidity and regular dew formation. Spores are spread by wind over considerable distances, allowing rapid disease development under favourable conditions. Management includes planting resistant hybrids, monitoring crops during the growing season, maintaining balanced crop nutrition and applying fungicides only when disease thresholds justify treatment. Fungicide applications should follow resistance-management recommendations. In Germany, only fungicides authorised by the Federal Office of Consumer Protection and Food Safety (BVL) may legally be used.

source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-disease extension guidance
text: Bacterial spot of tomato and pepper is caused by Xanthomonas euvesicatoria, a bacterial pathogen that affects leaves, stems and fruits. Symptoms include small water-soaked spots that enlarge into dark brown to black lesions, often surrounded by yellow halos. Fruit infections produce raised, rough, scab-like lesions that reduce market quality. The disease spreads through infected seed, contaminated transplants, rain splash, irrigation water, tools and human handling. Warm temperatures and high humidity strongly favour disease development. Unlike fungal diseases, bacterial spot cannot be cured with fungicides once plants are infected. Management focuses on using certified disease-free seed and transplants, removing infected plants, disinfecting equipment, avoiding overhead irrigation and practising crop rotation. Copper-based bactericides may help suppress disease when applied preventively, although resistance can occur and efficacy is often limited. In Germany, only plant-protection products currently authorised by the Federal Office of Consumer Protection and Food Safety (BVL) may legally be used.

source: EPPO Global Database; university plant-virus extension guidance
text: Tomato mosaic is caused by Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV), a highly stable plant virus that infects tomatoes and several other solanaceous crops. Symptoms include light and dark green mosaic patterns on leaves, leaf distortion, stunted plant growth, reduced fruit size and yield, and uneven fruit ripening. The virus spreads primarily through infected seed, contaminated hands, tools, clothing and direct contact between plants rather than by airborne spores. It can survive for long periods on contaminated surfaces and plant debris. Unlike fungal or bacterial diseases, there is no chemical cure for Tomato mosaic virus once a plant is infected. Management relies on preventing infection by using certified virus-free seed, removing and destroying infected plants, disinfecting tools and equipment, washing hands before handling plants, controlling volunteer plants and weeds that may act as reservoirs, and planting resistant tomato varieties where available. Because ToMV is a viral disease, fungicides and bactericides are ineffective. In Germany, only plant-protection products currently authorised by the Federal Office of Consumer Protection and Food Safety (BVL) may legally be used where applicable.

crops: Tomato, Cucumber, Pepper, Strawberry, Bean and many other crops
source: EPPO Global Database; university integrated pest management guidance
text: Two-spotted spider mite infestation is caused by Tetranychus urticae, a tiny plant-feeding mite that attacks a wide range of greenhouse and field crops. Feeding damage appears as numerous tiny yellow or white speckles on leaves, which gradually become bronzed, dry and may eventually fall. Heavy infestations produce fine silk webbing on leaves and stems and can significantly reduce plant growth, fruit quality and yield. The pest develops rapidly under warm, dry conditions and reproduces quickly, allowing populations to increase within a short period. Spider mites spread through plant-to-plant contact, wind, clothing and infested plant material. Management includes regular crop monitoring, removing heavily infested leaves, increasing humidity where appropriate in protected cultivation, conserving or introducing predatory mites as biological control agents, and avoiding unnecessary insecticide applications that may kill beneficial organisms. Where chemical control is necessary, acaricides specifically registered for mite control should be used and rotated between modes of action to delay resistance. Fungicides are not effective against spider mites. In Germany, only plant-protection products currently authorised by the Federal Office of Consumer Protection and Food Safety (BVL) may legally be used.
